---
title: "Random Effects"
subtitle: "Treating unit heterogeneity as random --- and knowing when that is safe"
---

## Overview

The Panel Data module showed how fixed effects sweep away time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity by comparing each unit only to itself. That safety comes at a price: fixed effects discard all between-unit variation, cannot estimate the effect of anything that does not change over time, and can be very imprecise when within-unit variation is scarce.

Random effects offer a different bargain. Instead of treating each unit's intercept as a separate parameter to be differenced away, they treat it as a *random draw* from a distribution. If that unit effect is genuinely uncorrelated with the regressors, random effects are not only consistent but *more efficient* than fixed effects --- and they can recover the effects of time-invariant variables. If that assumption fails, they are biased.

This module develops the random effects estimator as feasible GLS, confronts the central fixed-versus-random decision through the bias--efficiency tradeoff and the Hausman test, and shows how the Mundlak device buys fixed-effects robustness inside a random-effects framework. It is also the conceptual bridge to multilevel modelling.

## Pages in This Module

- [The Random Effects Model](random-effects-model.qmd) --- Treating unit heterogeneity as random.
- [Fixed versus Random Effects](fe-vs-re.qmd) --- The bias--efficiency tradeoff and the Hausman test.
- [Correlated Random Effects: The Mundlak Device](correlated-re-mundlak.qmd) --- Getting fixed-effects consistency inside a random-effects model.
